behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: The sorting logic could be tested in isolation (Unit), but the real failure boundary involves file discovery and order preservation through the application layer. A pure mock could hide bugs in filename parsing or filesystem listing order differences across platforms. Integration testing with real temporary files ensures both the discovery and ordering work end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with .sql files named 001_*.sql, 002_*.sql, 003_*.sql. Run the migration runner against a test database. Verify via the _migrations table that migrations were recorded in ascending numeric order, regardless of filesystem listing order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: The idempotency check depends on the _migrations table existing and being queried correctly. A mocked database would hide the failure—mocks could always return 'not applied' or the check could be missing entirely. Only a real database with a persistent _migrations table catches the failure that matters: whether a second run actually skips previously applied migrations.
behaviors.test_description: Run the migration runner once with a migration file, verify it applied. Drop and recreate the test database connection. Run the same migration runner again. Query the _migrations table and verify the migration appears exactly once (not twice).

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Rollback semantics are database-specific and cannot be meaningfully tested with mocks. A mocked rollback would always succeed, hiding bugs where the runner doesn't actually roll back or doesn't stop processing. Real database transaction semantics are required to verify that a constraint violation or syntax error within a migration truly rolls back its effects and halts subsequent migrations.
behaviors.test_description: Create two migration files: 001_valid.sql (valid statement) and 002_invalid.sql (syntax error or constraint violation). Run the migration runner. Verify: (1) the 001_valid migration's side effects exist in the database, (2) the 002_invalid migration did not run or was rolled back, (3) _migrations table has 001_valid but not 002_invalid, (4) no further migrations after 002 are applied.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Race conditions and locking semantics cannot be tested with mocks or even single-connection integration tests. The real failure is a race condition where both processes attempt to apply the same migration concurrently. Only real concurrent processes (or at least concurrent connections with realistic timing) against a real database with its actual locking behavior will expose missing synchronization, duplicate key errors, or lost updates.
behaviors.test_description: Start two concurrent instances of the migration runner pointing to the same test database at nearly identical times (or with coordinated barrier timing). Each instance should have its own connection. Run migrations with a moderate delay between them. Verify via the _migrations table that each migration is recorded exactly once, and inspect database logs or error handling to confirm no duplicate key violations or race conditions occurred.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: The table creation logic must execute and the schema must be correct. Mocks could hide missing table creation code or incorrect schema. The real failure boundary is whether a fresh database actually ends up with a valid _migrations table before migrations are applied. This requires real database schema operations and verification.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a clean test database (no _migrations table). Run the migration runner with at least one migration file. Before and after the run, query the database to verify: (1) _migrations table does not exist initially, (2) _migrations table exists after the run, (3) the migration is recorded in the table, (4) the table schema is correct (columns for migration name, applied_at, etc.).
